story:
I haul in the big box of dresses and jewelry for the ball and shut the door behind me. My foster mom, Claudia, and my two sisters bustle in, teary and excited. You’d think I’m Cinderella, overworked and unloved. I’m not. I may be Ella, but I’m no Cinderella. My foster mother loves me like her own, my sisters adore me, and our little family looks out for each other. We live in Silvania, a small town ruled by the Kennings. The King and Queen are my mom’s clients—she’s their personal painter—and their friends, too.

“You should come to the party,” my middle sister, Talia, urges. I frown. I don’t go to the palace anymore because of the prince. Chace and I do not get along. Charming, right? The first time we met, he’d just come back from military service and I was helping Mom with their portraits. My heel slipped off, he called me clumsy, and I stomped away. The second time, we somehow ended up on a blind date. It was a catastrophe. Nothing matched—interests, humor, patience—and we mutually vowed never again.

That evening, I help my mom and sisters get ready and send them off with Mr. Lund, our head butler, in my car. I stay home, sink into a rom-com, and am just about to reach the kissy climax when there’s a knock near midnight.

“Who is it?” I yell, not moving.

“Ella, open the door.”

No way. Chace. “You hate me. Why should I?”

He grumbles. “My car broke down. I’m already late. I have a ball to attend. I’m the Crown Prince, in case you forgot. My phone died.”

“Tragic,” I say. But I open the door. He’s sweaty, tired, and somehow still annoyingly handsome. He brushes mud from his tux bag. “I need to change. Then I need your car.”

“Mom and my sisters took it.”

He groans. I point him to the spare room to change. He comes back in a navy tux that really shouldn’t look that good. “Why aren’t you at the ball?” he asks.

“You should be grateful I don’t visit the palace because of you. Otherwise who would be rescuing you right now?”

He checks the time. “It’s almost midnight. The Midnight Dance—my first dance of the year.”

“Call someone.”

“I’m not admitting I’m late.” He’s in my house, but somehow I feel hijacked. “We have a bike,” I say. “My uncle left it.”

His face lights up. “You’re a lifesaver. Give me the keys—and go get dressed.”

“What?”

“If you come with me, everyone will forget that I’m late. Consider it my apology. I’ll save my first dance for you. Only you. We can start fresh.”

It’s ridiculous. I’m bored. “Fine.”

I throw him the keys and race upstairs. I slip into the light blue, mid-length gown my sisters made me order, brush my brunette hair and leave it down, and step into my glassy heels. When I come out, his eyes widen. “You look… wow.” He helps me onto the bike, tells me to hold tight, and we fly through the quiet streets.

We arrive ten minutes before midnight. He tugs me along and my heel slips—déjà vu. He kneels, fastens it properly, and whispers a real “Sorry.”

“Prince Chace and Miss Ella—” the guard announces, baffled, as we rush in. The royal advisor calls for the Midnight Dance. The King and Queen glance at us, half confused, half pleased.

The clock strikes twelve. Chace offers me his hand. “Ella, will you give me my first dance of the year?”

I take it. “Yes, my prince.” The crowd erupts in cheers. Our families watch, content, as he glides me across the floor. I’m not Cinderella, but tonight is still my fairy-tale moment.
